collocations.definitionEn: To be taught knowledge and skills.
collocations.exampleEn: Every child should receive a good education.

collocations.definitionEn: A growing effect on something.
collocations.exampleEn: Artificial intelligence is having an increasing impact on jobs.

collocations.definitionEn: A method that works well.
collocations.exampleEn: Group projects are an effective approach to teaching teamwork.

collocations.definitionEn: A practical answer that can work.
collocations.exampleEn: Renewable energy is a viable solution to climate change.

collocations.definitionEn: Today's communities and how they function.
collocations.exampleEn: Modern society relies heavily on technology in daily life.

collocations.definitionEn: The business activity of a particular area.
collocations.exampleEn: Tourism plays a vital role in the local economy.

collocations.definitionEn: The main national authority.
collocations.exampleEn: The central government has introduced new tax policies.

collocations.definitionEn: Nations with lower industrial development.
collocations.exampleEn: Many developing countries face serious environmental challenges.

collocations.definitionEn: Industrially advanced nations.
collocations.exampleEn: Developed countries should help reduce global poverty.

collocations.definitionEn: A small or restricted supply of materials.
collocations.exampleEn: Schools with limited resources struggle to provide modern equipment.

collocations.definitionEn: A promising and hopeful future.
collocations.exampleEn: Education gives young people a bright future.

collocations.definitionEn: To stop trying without much effort.
collocations.exampleEn: Successful people do not give up easily when facing problems.

collocations.definitionEn: To make a strong effort.
collocations.exampleEn: You need to try hard to improve your speaking score.

collocations.definitionEn: To take classes in a foreign country.
collocations.exampleEn: Many students choose to study abroad for better opportunities.

collocations.definitionEn: To read without making any sound.
collocations.exampleEn: Students were asked to read silently for twenty minutes.

collocations.definitionEn: To study very seriously for long periods.
collocations.exampleEn: She studied intensively for three months before the exam.

collocations.definitionEn: To want something very much.
collocations.exampleEn: He wanted badly to win the gold medal at the Olympics.

collocations.definitionEn: Required only by rules.
collocations.exampleEn: A second car is not strictly necessary in our family.

collocations.definitionEn: Connected in a strong way.
collocations.exampleEn: Sleep and mental health are closely related.

collocations.definitionEn: With no cost at all.
collocations.exampleEn: Many online courses are now completely free for everyone.

collocations.definitionEn: Publicly expressing disapproval.
collocations.exampleEn: Citizens are openly critical of the new transport policy.

collocations.definitionEn: Having clear and personal accountability.
collocations.exampleEn: Parents are directly responsible for their children's behaviour.

collocations.definitionEn: The conditions where people work.
collocations.exampleEn: A positive working environment improves productivity.

collocations.definitionEn: The level of people active in the labour market.
collocations.exampleEn: Female workforce participation has grown rapidly.

collocations.definitionEn: Differences in how much people earn.
collocations.exampleEn: Income inequality has increased in many countries.

collocations.definitionEn: A network for moving people and goods.
collocations.exampleEn: London has one of the largest transport systems in Europe.

collocations.definitionEn: The structure of schools and universities.
collocations.exampleEn: The Finnish education system is admired around the world.

collocations.definitionEn: Professional looking after of young children.
collocations.exampleEn: Affordable childcare services help mothers return to work.

collocations.definitionEn: The number of births per population.
collocations.exampleEn: The birth rate has fallen in many developed countries.

collocations.definitionEn: Laws restricting access to firearms.
collocations.exampleEn: Stricter gun control could reduce violent crime.

collocations.definitionEn: Violence within the home.
collocations.exampleEn: Domestic violence remains a serious social problem.

collocations.definitionEn: A fast rise in temperature.
collocations.exampleEn: Scientists are concerned about the rapid warming of the oceans.

collocations.definitionEn: The necessity to alter something.
collocations.exampleEn: There is a clear need for change in the welfare system.

collocations.definitionEn: The decline in the variety of living things.
collocations.exampleEn: Loss of biodiversity affects entire ecosystems worldwide.

collocations.definitionEn: Evidence that things are improving.
collocations.exampleEn: There are encouraging signs of progress in renewable energy.

collocations.definitionEn: How hard something is.
collocations.exampleEn: Each task has a different degree of difficulty in the exam.

collocations.definitionEn: An optimistic way of thinking.
collocations.exampleEn: A positive attitude is essential for overcoming challenges.

collocations.definitionEn: Conditions free from danger.
collocations.exampleEn: Children deserve to grow up in a safe environment.

collocations.definitionEn: The same chances given to everyone.
collocations.exampleEn: Schools must provide equal opportunity to all students.

collocations.definitionEn: Considering all parts of something together.
collocations.exampleEn: Doctors are taking a holistic approach to patient care.

collocations.definitionEn: An essential element.
collocations.exampleEn: Motivation is a key factor in academic success.

collocations.definitionEn: A difficulty found in many places.
collocations.exampleEn: Obesity is a widespread problem in many wealthy countries.

collocations.definitionEn: Materials that can be replaced naturally.
collocations.exampleEn: We should rely more on renewable resources like wind and solar.

collocations.definitionEn: A persuasive reason or case.
collocations.exampleEn: He made a convincing argument for free public transport.

collocations.definitionEn: Special importance given to something.
collocations.exampleEn: The school places strong emphasis on creative thinking.

collocations.definitionEn: An effect lasting many years.
collocations.exampleEn: Plastic pollution has a long-term impact on marine life.

collocations.definitionEn: A large decrease.
collocations.exampleEn: There has been a considerable reduction in smoking rates.

collocations.definitionEn: Work done together by several parties.
collocations.exampleEn: Solving climate change requires a joint effort by all nations.

collocations.definitionEn: Very enthusiastic and determined.
collocations.exampleEn: Highly motivated employees usually achieve better results.

collocations.definitionEn: Mentally calm and balanced.
collocations.exampleEn: Children need emotionally stable parents to grow up confident.

collocations.definitionEn: To handle a situation in a successful way.
collocations.exampleEn: Good managers deal effectively with difficult situations.

collocations.definitionEn: To talk honestly without hiding feelings.
collocations.exampleEn: Teenagers should be able to speak openly with their parents.

collocations.definitionEn: To consider issues that affect the whole world.
collocations.exampleEn: Modern citizens must think globally and act locally.

collocations.definitionEn: To take action in your own area.
collocations.exampleEn: Even small communities can act locally to protect nature.

collocations.definitionEn: Pride in successfully completing something.
collocations.exampleEn: Finishing a marathon gives runners a great sense of achievement.

collocations.definitionEn: Not having the drive to do something.
collocations.exampleEn: Lack of motivation is a common problem for distance learners.

collocations.definitionEn: The job a person decides to do.
collocations.exampleEn: A wise choice of career can shape your whole life.

collocations.definitionEn: Spending money on schools and learning.
collocations.exampleEn: Investment in education brings long-term benefits to society.

collocations.definitionEn: A method for gaining knowledge.
collocations.exampleEn: Each student has their own approach to learning new material.

collocations.definitionEn: An extremely important function.
collocations.exampleEn: Parents play a crucial role in shaping a child's values.
